data.title: Denison Mines: A High-Quality Uranium Project With Execution-Driven Upside
data.description:
Denison Mines (DNN) has a really long history in uranium mining through its predecessors, going back to the 1950s. In the last few years, with the announcement of the results of the pre-feasibility study of the Phoenix deposit in its Wheeler River project, Denison is now once again on the path to becoming a full-fledged uranium producer. Construction is about to begin. First production should happen in mid-2028.

The Phoenix ISR project is a high-grade, low-cost asset delivering ~73% IRR and rapid payback. All of these things have suddenly made investors notice DNN stock, and it has gone up significantly in the last one year.

Business Overview

Denison’s assets are mainly in Canada’s Athabasca Basin, which have some of the highest grade uranium in the world. While Denison has several exploration and development projects, the investment case now rests primarily on Wheeler River.

There are two key deposits here: Phoenix and Gryphon. Phoenix is the one that is near-term, with construction about to begin after they secured final investment approval. Gryphon is a second option, which will take more time, it being a conventional mining project, driving the company’s revenues beyond Phoenix.

Phoenix Project Economics

Key Project Metrics

(Note: costs are in US dollars, while capex and NPV are reported in Canadian dollars.)

What The Numbers Actually Say

Phoenix has a low cost structure of roughly $18 per pound all-in. That sits well below the current uranium price of $80-90. So the margin is $60+ per pound, placing the mine firmly in the lower end of the global cost curve.

A second feature is the front-loaded nature of the extraction. Approximately 41.9 million pounds, or nearly three-quarters of the total reserve, can be extracted within the first five years, at around 8.4 million pounds per year. This accelerates cash flow and reduces the impact of discounting. This is why both the NPV and the IRR are so high.

Here’s a quick example: take a base case uranium price of $68.89–78.36/lb. The project's AISC is $18.41/lb. That gives us a midpoint margin of around $55.2/lb. Multiplying with the annual product gives us $464M as the annual operating value for each of the first five years. During years 6-10, this drops to $166M. Discounting by 8%, we get roughly $1.85B for the first five years – nearly 80% – and ~$450M for the other five – just about 20% of the entire discounted value.

(Note: this is just an illustration of the cash-margin math to show how discounting works; not a full calculation of NPV, that would involve taxes, capex timings and so on, which management said comes to around $1.2 billion or ~C$1.56B).

The third, related point, is capital efficiency. Initial capex is around $600M, and NPV is $1.2B at conservative uranium prices. The estimated payback period is just one year. This is quite quick in the mining industry.

Sensitivity To Uranium Prices

(Note: costs are in US dollars, while NPV is reported in Canadian dollars.)

The economics are clearly sensitive to uranium prices, but even at more conservative assumptions, the project remains strongly profitable.

Why This Project Is Different

Phoenix stands out in two ways.

The first is grade, which, at ~11.7% U₃O₈, is an exceptionally high-grade deposit, even by Athabasca Basin standards. If ore is high grade, obviously, that translates directly into stronger margins and quicker capital recovery.

The second aspect is the mining method. This is an ISR (in-situ) operation, meaning there’s no mining and hauling rock, simply leaching and pumping through permeable layers. That also translates to lower capital requirement and lower operating costs.

The interesting thing is the fact that these two factors combine here. ISR is normally used in lower-grade deposits, whereas high-grade Athabasca projects typically need conventional mining methods. That is because lower-grade deposits are usually found in permeable rocks with spread-out orebody, like in the US or Kazakhstan. Here, Denison is using artificial methods to make the rock permeable so they can use ISR.

ISR has not been widely used in the Athabasca basin - indeed, Phoenix would be the first ISR use in Canada. However, if successful, this approach could create a new sort of cost profile for high-grade uranium deposits. This is a big reason the market assigns premium multiples to DNN.

Quick note on ISR

The way Phoenix uses ISR is not to address a mining problem but a fluid flow problem. Rather than digging and hauling rock, Denison will inject a lixiviant solution (a liquid solvent used in hydrology) into the orebody through wellfields. This will dissolve the uranium in situ, and it will be recovered through extraction wells. The FS has said that the rock there has sufficient permeability and hydraulic connectivity. Although the confirmation is modeled with limited test data, they have standardly used actual drilling and field testing.

The Athabasca basin is not naturally suited for ISR, so Denison is using this artificial strategy. This could potentially make Phoenix very cost efficient, and Denison may be able to expand the same expertise into their other assets in the basin.

Valuation

Using the first five years of production and the numbers we discussed earlier, we get the following operating picture:

Given its ~$600M initial capex, these annual run-rates for revenue and EBITDA are substantial, and show the project’s capacity to produce strong operating cash flow.

Now we come to the project NPV, the one figure that best represents the project’s value. This is:

  • ~C$1.57B at ~$70 uranium

  • ~C$1.9B at ~$85 uranium

  • ~C$2.35B at ~$100 uranium

This range shows that while the asset is strong, it is also sensitive to uranium prices.

Uranium price is the key external variable here. As we have seen, it is currently around ~$85/lb. Over the past year, it went up to ~$100/lb before pulling back and setting at $85.

At this level, Phoenix’s economics align with the mid-case scenario outlined above. We get an NPV of $1.9B, and with AISC at ~$18, we get strong margins. Importantly, this project does not need too high price assumptions. Even at ~$70 uranium, the feasibility study gives us an NPV of $1.57B, which is still economically robust given its capex requirements. However, valuation remains sensitive to pricing of uranium, as the difference between $70 and $100 is nearly $800M.

So Phoenix can be a very attractive project operationally. However, the current market cap of $3.26B creates a huge gap between operating quality and current equity value.

The first item we have to fix is currency. Denison discloses project NPVs in Canadian dollars, while the stock market cap here is in USD. Converting those figures roughly into US dollars (CAD$1=0.72USD), we get US$1.13B, US$1.40B, and US$1.60B, respectively. That means, the stock is trading at a very large premium to Phoenix’s own NPV. At today’s $3.26B market cap, investors are paying roughly thrice Denison’s 95% share of Phoenix NPV at $70 uranium. Even at $100 uranium, that comes to nearly 2x.

However, the market is not pricing DNN on Phoenix alone. They also have ~$150M of physical uranium holding, cash and investments of ~$393M; and the Gryphon deposit. It has a ~C$864M post-tax NPV at PFS level.

Running the calculation, if we assume a 50% discount to Gryphon NPV, uranium at ~$85, an SOTP valuation of DNN comes to around $2.1–2.3B. That still leaves a ~$1.0-1.2B gap to the current $3.26B market cap.

This gap comes from reasonable forward expectations rather than current asset value. Specifically, this assumes a higher uranium price, given that the Feasibility reports typically use conservative prices so that the NPVs derived therein may tend to be on the lower side. Secondly, the market is paying a premium for the ISR execution. If successful, this would be a great way to lower production costs at scale for all its future projects, which justifies a higher multiple. Thirdly, portfolio assets are not being priced at their current, heavily discounted NPVs, but on their potential to extend production for a long time.

Risks

The key risk here is the ISR mining method. It has never been used before in this area. Much of the value rests on its proper execution. It is true that the FS report supports ISR. However, if ISR does not work, the mine may still work, but the market may need to reprice.

The project is highly sensitive to uranium prices. Even though Phoenix remains profitable at lower prices, lower prices will impact NPV, return profile, and consequently the valuation premium applied.

Another risk is that the project is entering a multi-year construction phase. First production only begins in mid-2028. Our modeled costs may be substantially increased by delays in construction.

Conclusion

Denison has transitioned from a speculative uranium play to one with a major asset about to begin construction, and a defined path towards becoming a profitable producer. Phoenix is compelling with its potentially strong margins and rapid payback. Its high IRR and the uniqueness of using ISR also makes it attractive.

At the same time, the valuation already reflects some of this strength. Investors are paying not for Phoenix as it is today, or even for Phoenix alone, but for a very solid execution of the entire company.

Considering all these, I consider DNN a cautious buy at current prices, with significant upside over the next two years.

data.title: Intel Finds A Foundry Customer In Tesla - Diverging Valuations & Fundamentals
data.description:
I previously rated Intel Corporation (INTC) as a Hold in November 2025, attributed to the management's mixed forward guidance and their likely to be underwhelming return prospects in the intermediate term.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Hold rating for the INTC stock, with the notable divergence between its valuations and its mixed fundamentals triggering a mixed return/risk reward profile.

INTC Enjoys Another Recovery Tailwind

INTC bulls have proven my prior Hold rating wrong indeed, as observed in the stock's outsized +50.7% rally compared to the wider market at -0.3%, with a similar breakout also observed in its semiconductor peers in varying degrees.

1. Terafab Opportunities

Much of INTC's tailwinds is attributed to the legacy semiconductor company joining "the Terafab project alongside billionaire Elon Musk's companies SpaceX (SPACE), xAI (X.AI), and Tesla (TSLA) to help refactor silicon fab technology."

INTC's role will be "to design, fabricate, and package ultra-high-performance chips at scale," with it expected to drive their future renewal cadence beyond their prior GPU demand headwinds and their ongoing x86 market share erosion to 70.8% in Q4'25 (-4.6 points YoY).

Assuming a successful, long-term partnership, it goes without saying that INTC has found a great customer in TSLA and SpaceX, with it likely to boost the former's foundry prospects beyond the current internal consumption.

This is attributed to TSLA's well diversified capabilities across:

  • automotive - with 2.35M of installed vehicle production capacity globally (including Cybertruck),
  • autonomous - with 1.1M of paid FSD customers globally as of FQ4'25 (+37.5% YoY), and
  • robotics - with a "planned capacity of 1 million robots per year,"

with it building upon their Starlink ambitions of putting 1M satellites in space for data center/AI purposes, compared to the current 10K active satellites in orbit as of March 17, 2026.

These underscore the immense amount of compute/memory chips and packaging capabilities required for TSLA/SpaceX (where Starlink is embedded) to realize their ambitions.

The same has been highlighted by Elon Musk, at which the Terafab project is expected to deliver the eventual production capacity of 1TW annually, up by "50 times the current global compute supply of around 20 GW," with it presenting great tailwinds for INTC as TSLA/SpaceX's strategic foundry partner.

2. Terafab Risks

On the other hand, it remains to be seen how the Terafab partnership/monetization opportunity may be structured, attributed to the typically long lead time for planning, construction, fit out, and lithography calibration for semiconductor manufacturing facilities.

For reference, the world largest foundry by market share, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), previously broke ground in Arizona in 2021 and only commenced volume production in H1'25.

Pending further clarity on Terafab's timeline, I urge readers to temper their expectations indeed, since INTC's near-term prospects are still linked to their legacy x86 capabilities.

At the same time, while INTC has finally achieved volume production for Panther Lake (beyond the prior yield issues), it remains to be seen when INTC may achieve break even/profitability in the foundry segment, as observed in the operating margins of -55.7% in FQ4'25 (-3.9 points YoY).

Based on the management's last commentary in the Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference 2026, the management has hinted at volume production only by 2028/2029 for external customers, with it apparent that the foundry's top/bottom-line performance are likely to underwhelm for a little longer.

3. INTC's Premium Valuations Diverge From Fundamentals

With INTC expected to report their FQ1'26 earnings call on April 23, 2026, I urge readers to compare their upcoming performance against the previously offered guidance at:

  • FQ1'26 revenues of $12.2B (-10.9% QoQ/-3.9% YoY) and
  • FQ1'26 adj EPS at $0 (compared to a quarter ago at $0.15 and a year ago at $0.13),

since these numbers have yet to inspire confidence.

Otherwise, the consensus forward estimates appear rather optimistic, with INTC expected to report a top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +5.4%/+49.8% through FY2028, with it underscoring the market's confidence surrounding their ability to transition beyond the prior gross margin/foundry headwinds in FY2023/FY2024.

Otherwise, thanks to the +194% stock price rally from the 52 week lows and the lowered consensus forward estimates, it is unsurprising that INTC is expensive at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 103.23x, up notably from the 5Y mean of 53.50, the 10Y mean of 27.13x, and the sector median of 21.76x.

Based on the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +49.8% and the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 103.23x, it is apparent that INTC remains inflated at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.07x, compared to the sector median of 1.32x.

The same conclusion may also be derived when comparing INTC's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 103.23x and my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 10.32x to its direct AI accelerator peers, including:

  • general - Nvidia (NVDA) at 21.42x/0.52x,
  • general - Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) at 33.04x/0.63x,
  • custom - Broadcom (AVGO) at 27.70x/0.57x, and
  • custom - Marvell (MRVL) at 28.69x/0.76x, respectively,

with it apparent that INTC's mixed fundamentals have notably diverged from its expensive valuations, while offering interested readers a minimal margin of safety.

Part of the premium may be attributed to INTC's lower net debt levels at $9.16B in FY2025 (+67.2% YoY) and a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 0.62x (compared to a year ago at 2.51x), partly aided by the numerous cash injection by the US government/Softbank/NVDA.

Combined with the projected improvement in their performance metrics over the next few quarters, I am of the opinion that the management remains well capitalized to "rebuilding this iconic American company and increasing the long-term value for our shareholders."

INTC Diverging Valuations/Fundamentals Warrant A Hold Rating

For now, INTC has had an outsized H2'25 stock price recovery to retest the resistance levels of $52s by the time of writing, as the market rejoices at their long-term reversal prospects through the new management team's capital efficiency and the new Terafab partnership.

Readers may also want to note that INTC is notably overbought across the inflated RSIs and trading volumes, with it uncertain if the stock may be able to break out of the $52s levels attributed to the premium priced into its valuations.

Despite the supposedly decent sales performance well balancing their lumpy adj EPS performance, it goes without saying that INTC remains expensive at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 5.14x and my estimated FY2028 EV/Sales valuations of 4.64x, compared to the 5Y mean of 2.97x and the sector median of 2.86x.

This is based on the current Enterprise Value of $286.95B and the consensus FY2028 revenue estimates of $61.81B.

Given the reduced margin of safety from the pulled forward upside potential and the expensive valuations along with the likely to be lumpy bottom-line performance as they ramp up their foundry capabilities, I prefer to maintain my current Hold rating for the INTC stock here.

It may be more prudent to stand on the sidelines and hear out the management's commentaries surrounding Terafab in the upcoming FQ1'26 earnings call.

data.title: Disco Corporation: Spotlight On Above-Guidance Q4 Numbers
data.description:
I'm assigning a 'Buy' rating to Disco Corporation (DSCSY) (DISPF) (6146.T).

DSCSY's 4th quarter preliminary numbers surpassed the management guidance. This suggests that the chances of an earnings beat in late-April have increased. In the long run, the company benefits from multiple structural technology-related trends.

Corporate Overview

DSCSY is described by "Karreta Advisors" as a "leader in slicing and dicing machines used in the preparation of semiconductors with 70% market share."

The Firm's Role In Chip Fabrication

It derived 64% of FY24 (YE end-March 2025) sales from equipment such as dicers/grinders. The remainder was attributable to consumables (22%) and maintenance parts (14%).

Asia ex-Japan, North America, and Europe contributed 66%, 15%, and 9% of DSCSY's turnover during the same period. Its Japanese home market accounted for one-tenth of the group-level figure.

Unconsolidated Quarterly Metrics Exceeded Expectations

The company published its latest operational update on April 6.

DSCSY registered a parent-only topline of ¥105B for Jan-Mar '26. This translated into sequential and year-on-year growth rates of +19% and +2%, respectively. I'm encouraged that the 4QFY25 print was also 12.9% better than management's earlier guide.

At Monday's analyst call (S&P Capital IQ transcript), it explained that accelerated acceptance testing pulled revenue recognition forward.

Separately, DSCSY's recent three-month deliveries jumped 28% YoY to ¥98B which is impressive in my view. That's ahead of the ¥96B internal forecast as well.

Demand for its "precision processing tools" or "consumables" surprised positively in 4QFY2025 according to the enterprise's briefing comments.

Favorable Read-Throughs For Upcoming Earnings Release

DSCSY will be revealing the full-entity fourth quarter financials on April 22.

I believe that the sell-side is too conservative. The consensus currently anticipates a 1.9% YoY top-line increase and flattish "Operating Profit/OP" for 4QFY25.

My bet is on the company achieving January-March 2026 sales of ¥126B (105*1.2) implying a superior +4.4% expansion. I've assumed a consolidated-to-parent multiple of 1.20 times. That's largely consistent with DSCSY's 1.195x mean FY23/24 ratio.

In the previous section, I drew attention to DSCSY's above-expectations Q4 tools-related shipments. In my opinion, this bodes well for its future profitability. You would think that consumables should have higher "Gross Profit Margins" or "GPMs" than hardware.

The analysts are now estimating that the group's 4QFY2025 GPM contracts 1.1ppts QoQ and rises modestly by 50bps YoY. This appears to be overly cautious, taking into account the accretive mix shift.

Also, the management team emphasized at the Apr 6 meeting that its factory utilization is still reasonably high. It also noted the deployment of a hundred headquarters employees to production floors for a half-year period. These disclosures make me optimistic about DSCSY's late-Apr release.

Long Runway Ahead

Looking beyond the quarter, the firm's outlook remains promising.

"HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) spend is projected to compound at roughly 42% annually to approach $100 billion by 2028" as per a late-Mar "Global X ETFs" article.

I see DSCSY as a huge beneficiary of this trend. Jefferies (JEF) issued a report (not publicly available) titled "Earnings Uptrends" on Feb 3, 2026. JEF's research estimates that HBM represents roughly seven-tenths of the company's GenAI-related deliveries.

Also, "BE Semiconductor Industries" (BESIY) highlighted HBM's shift from "12-Hi" to "16-Hi" chip layers at its earnings call. That should warrant more intensive grinding on a per-wafer basis, which is good for Disco's future equipment demand.

On the other hand, Applied Materials, Inc. (AMAT) recently indicated that "customers want new packaging substrates as large as possible to connect more GPUs."

My take is that there will be a need for DSCSY to introduce specialized tools to accommodate the increase in form factors. This will in turn boost the company's overall "Average Selling Prices" or "ASPs."

Last month, the management team organized a call with the sell-side. They mentioned "DISCO is getting long-term forecasts through 2027 for some customers." These remarks were obtained from Morgan Stanley's (MS) March 5 note "President Meeting Summary." This sends me a strong message that the enterprise has a visible growth trajectory over a longer horizon.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Bear-Case Scenarios

A 4QFY2025 earnings miss could lead to a substantial pullback in DSCSY's shares. That might be driven by a spike in expenses or a lower-than-anticipated proportion of high-margin consumable sales.

Disco's future growth may disappoint investors if it doesn't have sufficient capacity to meet robust client demand. As outlined above, the firm has already redeployed some of its staff to deal with the labor crunch.

My Conclusion

I like this name as a potential investment. Its prospects for the near-term and long-run are favorable.

DSCSY trades at a forward FY2027 EBITDA multiple of 23x as per S&P Capital IQ. That's on par with its 3-year trading mean of 22.8 times.

The company is dominant in the grinders/dicers segment and boasts solid growth potential. My opinion is that the stock's current valuations, being aligned with historical averages, are reasonably attractive.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Energy Vault: Decarbonisation And AI Driving Energy Storage Ramp Up
data.description:
While Energy Vault's (NRGV) rapid rally has cooled from its 52-week high, the utility-scale energy storage company is still underway with a rapid buildup of its order book and corresponding revenue growth. Revenue for its fiscal 2025 fourth quarter came in at $153.3 million, up a material 358% over its year-ago comp and a beat by $740,000 on consensus estimates. Critically, NRGV saw its contract backlog grow to $1.3 billion as of the end of the fourth quarter, adding $380 million of orders sequentially and up 3x from the year-ago comp. The company's product offerings include its proprietary battery, gravity, and green hydrogen energy storage technology stacks sold to a range of customers, including industrial energy users, utilities, and independent power producers ("IPPs").

NRGV was originally listed on the NYSE via a special purpose acquisition company in 2022, offering only a novel gravity-based long-duration energy storage ("LDES") technology stack. This was an intense solution that would use excess renewable energy to lift huge composite blocks that would then be dropped for dispatchable energy, smoothing out the peaks and troughs of intermittent renewable energy generation. NRGV has since expanded its technology portfolio to deliver short, long, and ultra-long duration energy storage solutions.

The bullish thesis here comes from the ongoing transition to renewables to limit carbon emissions and load growth as the global electric grid takes on more data centers every year on the back of AI demand for compute. U.S. utility Duke Energy (DUK) is increasing its 5-year capital expenditure plan by $16 billion to meet higher load demand through to the end of the decade, with Southern Energy (SO) set to boost its capital spending plan by as much as $15 billion to meet roughly 8 gigawatts of additional load over the next six years. These spending plans are centered on new electricity generation, with solar and wind energy, and subsequent storage set to play a central role across utilities and IPPs. I last covered NRGV with a Hold rating in 2024.

Commercial Pipeline, Growth Guidance, And Downside Risk

NRGV's order backlog represents contracted but unrecognized revenue from third-party projects and services yet to be completed, from IP licensing agreements, and from tolling arrangements for projects operated by NRGV or affiliates. This order backlog, around 3 GWh, essentially forms downstream revenue that NRGV will progressively convert to revenue through the next few years. The year-over-year backlog increase of around 325% represents solid momentum, with NRGV also pushing forward with an "own and operate" strategy in 2025 that placed two owned energy storage systems into commercial operation. GAAP gross profit reached $31.6 million during the fourth quarter, with gross profit margin coming in at 23.6% for the full fiscal year 2025. This was a significant increase from 13.4% in NRGV's fiscal 2024.

The company generated a GAAP net loss of $20.7 million during the fourth quarter, a material improvement from a loss of $61.8 million in the year-ago comp as higher revenue generation from order book conversion filtered through to the bottom line. NRGV's cash from operations remains negative at $6.57 millon during the quarter, on the back of positive adjusted EBITDA of $9.8 million. The company is guiding for 2026 revenue to come in between $225 million and $300 million, growth of around 28.8% over 2025 at the midpoint of the range. This means NRGV at its current $520 million market cap is trading at around a 2x multiple to this midpoint.

For some context, Fluence Energy (FLNC) is trading at a multiple of 0.8x, and its one of the largest energy storage companies by revenue with year-over-year growth of 154% recorded for its most recently reported earnings quarter. This places NRGV's current multiple in a negative context as growth for 2026 is set to be lower than FLNC's, but with the company trading hands at a markedly larger multiple. The risk here is continued multiple compression, with NRGV's trailing twelve months price-to-sales multiple down from as high as 9x at the end of last year. The company ended the fourth quarter with cash and cash equivalents of $103.4 million, up around 67% sequentially. NRGV is guiding for cash as of the end of 2026 after debt repayment and a convertible debt offering to be in the range of $150 million to $200 million. Hence, the company's cash runway, especially against modest cash burn from operations, runs quite deep.

Conclusion

NRGV forms a possible play on the macrotrend of AI, with decarbonisation also driving sustained demand for renewables. However, AI animal spirits have become less euphoric from their 2025 level, and pick-and-shovel plays like NRGV have seen their stock prices come back down hard. The company's current multiple still keeps me on the sidelines, so I am rating NRGV as a Hold.

data.title: Palantir: This Could End Badly
data.description:
Palantir's (PLTR) stock has been losing value in recent months despite the fact that its business has continued to deliver strong results. In Q4 alone, the revenues increased by almost 70% Y/Y, and the expectations are that the growth rate will be over 60% this year. Yet the stock has not been able to recover to its November highs.

This is because the broader AI sentiment has shifted, the software sector has been crushed by fears of agentic AI disruption, and the macro environment has gotten worse as the Iran war pushes oil prices higher and keeps the Fed from cutting rates. In addition, the company’s co-founder, Peter Thiel, has been selling hundreds of millions of dollars' worth of shares in recent months. Considering all of this and the fact that the risks that the business faces have only increased since our latest coverage on the company that we published at the beginning of this year, we decided to continue to rate Palantir as a Sell.

The Sentiment Has Shifted

Our latest bearish article on Palantir was published a few weeks before the company reported its Q4 results. The results themselves turned out to be quite good. The revenue during the quarter was above expectations and reached $1.4 billion, while the earnings per share also didn’t disappoint the analysts. However, the market didn’t care much about those results.

The shares themselves started to lose value in November when the sentiment surrounding the AI-related stocks started to shift. We have highlighted this in our previous article from January. Then the shares took another major hit in February, right after the release of Palantir’s Q4 earnings report. During that time, Anthropic launched Claude Cowork, which is a set of AI agent tools designed to handle complex professional workflows. Following that, roughly $2 trillion was wiped from software stock valuations, while hedge funds made $24 billion shorting software stocks in a matter of weeks.

The thinking is that if AI agents can do the work of multiple employees, then companies don't need multiple software seats anymore, as one could be more than enough. In addition, organizations might ditch Palantir’s software and move to custom solutions with the help of LLMs if the pricing is better. Although Palantir's Ontology framework makes it harder to displace than a standard SaaS product, the company trades at over 40 times its forward sales. At that kind of valuation, even a perception of risk is enough to do damage.

What made the Anthropic situation even more relevant for Palantir is that it hit the company directly when another development happened shortly after. For a while, Anthropic’s LLM was the sole model cleared for Palantir’s classified Maven Smart Systems workflows at the Pentagon's highest security levels. When it was revealed that Anthropic’s Claude AI tool was used in the capture of Venezuela's President Maduro in January, concerns started to be raised about its usage. Later, Anthropic rejected the Pentagon's demand for unrestricted access to its models. The Pentagon then designated Anthropic a supply chain risk and ordered all contractors to stop using its products. For Palantir, this could mean rebuilding the prompts, agent chains, and evaluation pipelines that were specifically built around Claude. Reuters reported last month that the process could take months, and it could be challenging.

As a result of all of this, there’s a risk that Palantir’s future growth in government-related business could be limited until it finds a replacement for Anthropic. At the same time, there’s a risk that if AI agents become more scalable and customizable for commercial customers, then they might use them and no longer rely on third-party software providers like Palantir.

What is worse is that all of this is happening at a time when the broader market sentiment around AI stocks continues to aggressively shift. Earlier this month, Goldman Sachs data showed that hedge funds sold global stocks in March at the fastest pace in 13 years. When a selloff happens, usually stocks with the highest multiples are impacted the most. Given the aggressive multiples at which Palantir is trading, it won’t surprise us if the stock falls to even lower levels in the foreseeable future.

In addition to all of that, the macro environment also keeps getting worse. Because of the war in Iran, oil is already trading around $100 per barrel, and there’s a risk that the inflation rate could rise in the following months as a result of this. This could have a direct impact on Palantir’s business as well. After all, it could affect the economic activity and make Palantir miss the revenue or earnings expectations. If that happens, the stock could have a long way to fall considering the multiples at which it currently trades.

The Real Value Of Palantir

Considering all of the developments that are happening, we wanted to find out what Palantir’s intrinsic value is in the current environment. In our valuation model, we used a perpetual growth rate of 3%, which is similar to the historical inflation and GDP rate. Most of the financial data has been taken from the company’s latest earnings report. The discount rate in the model is 10.21%, which is the same as Palantir’s cost of equity rate. This is because the company has no long-term debt and very small total debt. When calculating the cost of equity, we used a risk-free rate of 4.30%, a beta of 1.67, and a market return rate of 7.84%.

When it comes to forecasting Palantir’s future growth, we expected the company to grow at 60% in 2026 in the previous model. The consensus now is that the growth will be slightly above 62%. As such, we’ve increased our growth rate assumptions to 65% since there’s a possibility that Palantir will be able to beat the expectations in the upcoming quarters. For all the other years in the model, we kept the growth rate at a pretty aggressive rate of 60%.

As for the EBIT margin, our previous model showed that in 2024 it was slightly above 10%, while this model shows that in 2025 it was already slightly above 30%. Considering this, we kept the margin in the upcoming years at 55%, which is achievable considering that, as a software-focused company, Palantir doesn’t have significant expenses in comparison to companies from other industries. The tax rate in the model is similar to the tax rate of other tech companies, which we covered in recent months. The assumptions for the remaining metrics are not far from Palantir’s historical performance.

This model shows that Palantir’s enterprise value is $239.5 billion, while its equity value is $246.44 billion. That translates to an intrinsic value of $95.76 per share. Even under pretty aggressive assumptions in this model, Palantir remains greatly overvalued right now. If the risks described earlier in this article begin to materialize, then it’s possible that the company’s growth rate will be lower than what we currently assume. That would make Palantir’s intrinsic value even lower than what our current calculations show.

Some Upside Could Still Be There

The bearish case against Palantir has never been about the business itself, and that hasn't changed. Despite all the issues, the business is still expected to grow quite aggressively in the following years. In addition, the net dollar retention of 139% shows that existing customers keep spending more. As such, there’s a possibility that the risk from the rise of AI agents could somehow be mitigated in the short term.

Also, despite the disagreements between the Pentagon and Anthropic, Palantir’s Maven system nevertheless was able to become an official program of record last month. As such, there’s a possibility that if the geopolitical instability continues to rise, Palantir's defense business could become even more valuable without Anthropic’s AI tools over time.

There's also a chance that the macro environment improves. If the Middle East situation calms down faster than expected and inflation doesn't spike the way some analysts fear, the risk-off mood could flip. Under such a scenario, Palantir’s stock at the very least would have the potential to rebound in the short term.

Final Thoughts

Palantir’s business has been performing well so far. Yet the stock has done nothing but fall in recent months. The SaaS sector is getting crushed by AI agent fears. The Anthropic fallout creates real near-term execution risk for Palantir’s Maven system. And the valuation, at over 100 times forward earnings, still assumes years of flawless execution in a market that is no longer willing to pay for that assumption. Because of all of this, we believe that giving Palantir's stock a rating of Sell makes the most sense in the current environment.

data.title: The U.S. Tariff Shock In 2025 Vs. 2026 - Same Negative Impact, Different Drivers
data.description:
By Ruben Dewitte, Economist

The rollout of new US tariffs in 2025 triggered months of uncertainty and market volatility. One year on, their impact is becoming clearer. Although the tariff hike was historical, its direct effect on the European economy proved more muted than initially feared. This not only reflected exemptions, tariff front-loading and strong US demand for pharmaceutical imports (especially from Ireland), but also the fact that US tariffs on many competing suppliers also rose sharply in 2025, temporarily preserving Europe’s competitive position on the US market. Still, higher tariffs, the stronger euro, and heightened uncertainty weighed visibly on EU‑US trade.

Looking ahead, the drivers of EU-US trade are shifting. The Turnberry deal locks in a 15% tariff rate for EU exports, while tariffs on competing countries are also set to converge towards 15% in 2026. Countries such as China (31.1% effective tariff rate in 2025), India (20.5%) and Indonesia (20.8%) will therefore face substantially lower tariff rates than in 2025, eroding the EU’s relative position on the US market. As a result, the drag on EU exports is likely to persist, driven less by uncertainty and tariff asymmetries and more by a deterioration in relative competitiveness.

US tariffs jumped sharply in 2025, but less than feared

US tariffs increased sharply in 2025. The effective tariff rate rose by 8.1 percentage points, a historic jump, but one that ultimately proved less severe than the most adverse scenarios had anticipated. This more benign outcome partly reflected tariff‑stacking rules and sector‑specific exemptions and partly the ability of exporters to adapt by rerouting trade flows or adjusting the composition of shipments to the US market.

For the EU, the effective tariff rate climbed to 8.5% in 2025, up by 7.2ppt compared with 2024. Despite the EU’s common external tariff, effective tariff levels varied across countries due to different export structures and access to exemptions. As such, France, Belgium, and the Netherlands faced roughly half the effective tariff burden of countries such as Germany, Italy or Spain.

Large differences in effectively applied tariff rates, even within the European Union

EU-US exports in 2025: Tariff front‑running, Irish outperformance and a sharp post‑tariff decline

EU exports to the US followed a highly uneven pattern in 2025. Ahead of Liberation Day, exporters rushed shipments across the Atlantic in anticipation of higher tariffs, temporarily boosting exports by up to 35% year-on-year. This front‑loading effect was particularly pronounced in Ireland, where strong US demand for weight loss‑related pharmaceutical products largely offset the negative impact of tariffs throughout the year.

Once higher tariffs took effect, however, EU‑US trade weakened markedly. EU exports to the US, excluding Ireland, fell by 4.1% in 2025, despite exports to the rest of the world increasing by around 1.5% last year. This sharp difference suggests that the tariff shock had a strong negative impact on EU‑US trade, even if it was temporarily masked by front‑loading and Ireland‑specific factors, and broader macroeconomic developments like the strengthening of the euro.

EU exports to the US surged ahead of tariff hikes but fell markedly once higher tariffs took effect

Tariff impact in 2025: Uncertainty amplifies the hit, competitiveness cushions the blow

Given these overlapping effects, what was the net impact of US tariffs on EU‑US trade in 2025? To answer this, we isolate and decompose the impact of US trade policy on EU trade into three channels using a regression framework.

  1. The direct tariff effect was immediate and sizeable. Following Liberation Day, higher tariffs triggered a strong fall in demand, depressing EU‑US trade.
  2. This negative impact was more than offset by the sharp increase in tariffs faced by competing suppliers on the US market, such as China and India. As a result, the EU effectively regained ground relative to the direct tariff shock.
  3. Tariffs also affected trade through non‑tariff channels. Heightened uncertainty initially encouraged tariff frontrunning, but later led to postponed investment and exports, as well as lower trade volumes due to rising administrative and compliance burdens.

Taken together, the direct tariff impact, shifts in relative competitiveness and uncertainty‑related effects point to a net reduction of around 4.2% in EU‑US exports in 2025. This estimate captures the pure impact of tariffs, abstracting from broader macroeconomic conditions. Yet, it aligns remarkably closely with the observed decline in EU exports to the US once the exceptional surge in Irish exports is excluded.

US tariffs are estimated to reduce EU‑US exports by more than 4% in both 2025 and 2026

More certainty, higher tariffs ahead

Against this backdrop, the endorsement of the Turnberry deal by the European Parliament marks a clear turning point in EU‑US trade relations (even if national ratification still has to follow). By ruling out the near‑term use of tariff coercion instruments, the agreement brings greater clarity and predictability. Fixing tariffs at 15% should reduce trade policy uncertainty and ease non‑tariff frictions, lowering compliance costs for firms exporting to the United States.

That clarity, however, comes at a price. Locking in a 15% tariff likely implies a higher effective tariff rate than the 8.5% observed in 2025. As a consequence, the EU’s relative competitive position is set to weaken in 2026 as Europe’s competitors in the US markets, like China, India, and Indonesia, will benefit relatively more from the US Supreme Court’s ruling and the rollback of IEEPA‑related tariffs.

Taken together, higher tariffs and a deteriorating relative competitive position will outweigh the benefits of increased certainty in 2026. Unlike in early 2025, exporters can no longer rely on tariff frontrunning to cushion the impact. Moreover, as firms gradually adjust and substitution possibilities increase, trade responses will intensify. As a result, EU export growth to the US is expected to slow further by around 4.6% in 2026 due to tariffs alone, abstracting from broader macroeconomic developments, even though the underlying drivers differ from those observed in 2025.

Overall, the tariff shock may have been less dramatic than initially feared based on headline numbers, but its economic damage is already evident and is likely to extend into 2026. Diversifying trade towards partners such as Mercosur and India is both a necessary and welcome policy response, but these markets still fall short of the depth of the US market. This reinforces the importance of strengthening the EU internal market as a key policy priority going forward.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Ares Capital: I'm Not Bearish Anymore But Still Have Concerns
data.description:
Performance assessment

My recent 'Sell' views on Ares Capital (ARCC) have worked out well:

Elevator pitch

I'm becoming less bearish now on ARCC, although I still see challenges:

  1. Strong deal flow bodes well for future NII
  2. Ares Capital's portfolio is prone to AI disruption via its software exposure
  3. ARCC still looks relatively richly valued vs. BDC comps
  4. ARCC is at monthly support, but there are no strong buyers yet

Strong deal flow bodes well for future NII

Fundings and commitments (1 step before funding) have been increasing sharply over the last couple of quarters:

And it is encouraging to see that the dollar value increase in commitments is not concentrated. In fact, it is comprised of a very diversified pool of 114 separate bets:

This higher deal flow is not only due to sector-wide tailwinds, but also Ares Capital's large scale (it is the largest BDC with the top holding weight in the Vaneck BDC Income ETF (BIZD)) and the resulting breadth and expertise depth that follows from that:

While the broader tailwinds of increasing market activity levels helped drive higher originations to new borrowers in the second half of the year, much of this growth also came from the continued expansion of our specialized industry verticals...

In 2025, our investment team reviewed nearly $1 trillion of potential investments, representing a 24% increase in the number of opportunities we reviewed relative to the prior year.

- CEO Kort Schnabel in the Q4 FY25 earnings call

These new investment commitments will eventually add to the investment portfolio from which net investment income [NII] can be generated. Hence, I expect an uptick from the currently stagnant levels of ~$350-370M:

Ares Capital's portfolio is prone to AI disruption via its software exposure

Almost a quarter of Ares Capital's $29.5B investment portfolio comes from the software sector:

This Software and Services mix is going to only increase as the latest backlog and pipeline mix (a leading indicator of future investments) shows a much higher 38% coming from this sector:

The problem is that many software companies are facing existential risks as AI agents become capable of doing their work:

Enterprise-ready agents are clearly reducing the barriers to entry for software development, including reducing the cost, which makes any assumptions of future growth very difficult to model and raises an unquantifiable threat in terms of future competition...

Customers pay for a licence, per user, and traditionally the business enjoys high stickiness and very high gross margins. But if an AI model can produce a comparable design in seconds at a fraction of the cost, or if a firm no longer needs the same number of designers, the threat to the seat becomes real.

- Global Equity Fund Manager David Osfield at Edentree (select professional investor to access the link)

This is why software ETFs such as the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) have corrected almost 33% since the start of Q4 CY25:

Of course, not all software companies are affected the same way. And ARCC's management argues that they focus on foundational, infrastructure-level software in regulated markets. These kinds of companies have a protective "data moat", similar to that of Palantir (PLTR) (although I suspect not as strong). And that this makes immediate AI replacement unlikely.

However, I think the risks may be a bit more skewed to the downside here. It would be ideal if Ares Capital disclosed their software portfolio in more granular detail. That would help quell some concerns if their reassurances are really valid.

ARCC still looks relatively richly valued vs. BDC comps

At a 1-yr fwd PE valuation of 9.34x, ARCC trades at a 16.3% premium to its BDC sectoral comps' median multiple of 8.03x:

I have taken the companies in the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) to form the peer set in the analysis above.

This kind of premium is much higher than the longer-term median of 6.3%. Hence, I would say ARCC is still richly valued vs. its BDC peers.

ARCC is at monthly support, but there are no strong buyers yet

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return, as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

ARCC Technical Analysis

ARCC's correction has now reached a major monthly support area. And there are some signs of the monthly support holding up due to the wick rejections. But there are no strong buyers present yet. So overall, I expect more sideways rollover to follow.

Takeaway & Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Neutral, as strong deal flow is offset by software exposure risks.
  • Valuation: Bearish due to relative overvaluation signals.
  • Technical Analysis: Neutral as it is resting at monthly support, but not bullish as there are no signs of buyers yet.
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Neutral/Hold.

data.title: Diebold Nixdorf: Index Inclusion Is Additional Validation Of Diebold Nixdorf's Turnaround
data.description:
This article serves as an update to my previous analysis of Diebold Nixdorf (DBD) that was published on September 23. I do not often write about companies in the tech sector, but DBD’s operations are connected to banking and financial services, two industries which I have covered in detail over the last year in Seeking Alpha. I rated Diebold Nixdorf as a Buy in my initial analysis, and the stock has gained almost 40% in about six and half months of time, outpacing the S&P 500 by over 37%. As part of the support for my previous rating, I noted that the company’s profitability, free cash flow, and profit margins were all trending in the right direction. DBD was also making progress on reducing its debt load which would help reduce future interest costs.

On April 7, Diebold Nixdorf announced that it would be added to the S&P SmallCap 600 Index on April 10. This has given the stock an addition boost this week as it creates buying pressure for index funds who now need to add DBD to their holdings. Fundamentally, this change does little for Diebold Nixdorf except give additional validity to a company that declared bankruptcy less than three years ago.

The better news for DBD shareholders is that progress continues to be made with the company’s operations, and expectations for FY 2026 seem to be getting stronger. When I last wrote about the company, earnings per share for this year were expected to be around $4.96. In just over half a year’s time, that number now stands at $5.49. Even with this week's index-related price spike, the stock still trades at a forward PE below 15. I think this week’s run-up in price may settle back down in the next week or so, but over the long-term, Diebold Nixdorf still looks like a buy.

Company Overview

Diebold Nixdorf is a retail technology company that has a history dating back over 160 years. The company’s first product was a safe, and today’s version of DBD is still heavily connected to safety and security. Perhaps best known for its production of ATM machines that started in the 1970’s, Diebold Nixdorf has also been connected to the start of drive-up banking, the first kiosk in quick service restaurants, and the first circular vault door. For more detail about the history of the company and its operations, readers are encouraged to look back at my first article from last September.

The current version of Diebold Nixdorf serves both the banking and retail industries with hardware and software to enhance customer experience and security. DBD has a presence in over 100 countries worldwide and employs approximately 20,000 people. The company reported $3.80 billion in revenue in FY 2025, a 1.5% increase from the previous year. A breakdown in the sources for that revenue is given below. As can be seen, the banking side of the business produces the largest share of DBD’s top line.

The company also reported a backlog of orders at the end of last year that totaled $733 million, a decrease of 7.2% from the end of 2024. This total includes product orders that are projected to be shipped or installed in the next 18 months. The backlog total does not include any advanced orders for its services business, which makes this piece of information somewhat less valuable for predicting exactly what the future for the company may hold.

At the end of 2025, Diebold Nixdorf reported total assets of $3.85 billion on its balance sheet, 8.8% more than at the end of 2024. Stockholders’ equity rose 18.3% in 2025 from $929.8 million to $1.10 billion. The total net income for the company last year equaled $94.6 million, which is a complete reversal from the $16.5 million that was lost in FY 2024.

DBD does not pay a dividend and stated in its last 10-K that it is unlikely to initiate any payouts in the near future. However, the company has been active in repurchasing shares over the last year. In February of 2025, the board authorized a $100 million stock buyback plan. In November of last year, it approved a program to repurchase an additional $200 million. This is not insignificant for a company that has a total market cap of just $2.7 billion. During FY 2025, DBD repurchased just over 2.3 million shares at a value of $128 million. The buyback activity for Q4 2025 is shown in the table below.

The Case for Diebold Nixdorf

There was a lot of good news to share in Diebold Nixdorf's Q4 2025 earnings call. In addition to the increase in revenue, the company reported a significant increase in both EBITDA and EBITDA margin on a year-over-year basis. EBITDA moved up 7.4% to $485 million, while EBITDA margin rose from 12.1% to 12.7%. Free cash flow for the year increased a whopping 120% as Q4 was the fifth consecutive quarter of positive free cash flow, a record for the company.

All of these items helped spur DBD to a substantial increase in its adjusted earnings per share. However, the 182% improvement shown in the graph above from $1.98 per share to $5.59 per share does come with a footnote. The amount reported included $1.08 of non-cash operational tax benefits. Nonetheless, even if those are pulled out, the remaining total of $4.51 is well above the $3.56 that was expected for the company when I wrote my last analysis last September.

Other financial trends for the company appear to be largely positive. Total revenue crossed the $1 billion mark in Q4 2025 and trended upward throughout the year. Diebold Nixdorf noted in its financial filings that it normally books about 45% of its revenue in the first half of the year, so increases in the second half are expected. Gross margins also trended upward last year, as the graph below shows. Operating expenses remained relatively constant over the last several quarters with operating expense as a percent of revenue dropping 1.2% from Q4 2024 to Q4 2025.

There are a number of reasons to believe that FY 2026 will look even better than FY 2025 for DBD. The company reported that it has built momentum in its banking operations with solutions that drive efficiency for local bank branches such as teller cash recyclers and fit-for-purpose ATMs that can be customized to meet the needs of the specific location. One of my initial concerns for the company was that digital payments and a push toward more cashless transactions could impact demand for ATMs, but the market for those devices is expected to rise in the US, albeit modestly, through 2033.

On the retail side, Diebold Nixdorf's new AI-powered solution, "Vynamic Smart Vision I Shrink Reduction" satisfies retailers' need to implement and deploy new technologies across multiple store locations and geographies. The software can monitor self-checkout lines and regular shopping lanes for mistakes and theft-like behavior. This saves businesses that use the software both time and money in the long run. This example is just one of the ways that the company has evolved to meet the needs of the retail industry and provide additional support for my Buy recommendation.

Risks to Consider

The debt load for Diebold Nixdorf is still substantial. Long-term debt on the company’s balance sheet at the end of 2025 totaled $938.5 million. The good news is that DBD’s ability to manage this debt and the costs associated with it seem to be improving. On September 19, Diebold Nixdorf received a credit upgrade from S&P Global Ratings from “B” to “B+”. In mid-December of last year, Moody's revised its rating from "B2" to "B1" with a stable outlook. While these improvements are a positive sign, the current ratings still place the company’s debt in categories considered highly speculative. Interest expenses pose a significant challenge to DBD’s long-term profitability. Investors are reasonable to proceed with caution on any investment in the company considering it filed for bankruptcy less than three years ago. Any slip up in the company’s recovery could quickly erode investor confidence in the stock.

Diebold Nixdorf operates in competitive market segments that depend on a healthy banking sector for orders for hardware and additional services. Declines in profits from banks, either in the US or overseas, could cause them to delay upgrading their assets and negatively impact DBD’s bottom line. Pricing competition could also affect profit margins for the company that have been trending positively over the last year. Diebold Nixdorf will have to continue to innovate both its hardware and software to stay competitive both in the US and overseas.

The company’s operations in foreign countries also exposes it to some currency risk. DBD does use derivatives to manage some risk that involves trade and investments in foreign subsidies. Diebold Nixdorf reports its earnings in US dollars, which has gained some traction in 2026 after dropping roughly 10% in 2025. DBD lost $44.1 million in currency exchange in FY 2025 after reporting a gain of $13.8 million in 2024.

Looking Ahead to Q1 and FY 2026

Diebold Nixdorf is expected to release its Q1 2026 earnings on May 7. The average estimate from the three analysts that cover the company calls for adjusted earnings per share of $0.64. That would be a significant improvement from the $0.09 that was reported in Q1 2025.

From my perspective, an earnings miss for the first three months of this year would not be a total surprise. The $0.09 from Q1 last year was $0.27 below expectations at that time. This year, supply chain issues and concerns around global trade that have arisen in the last month may have some impact on the company's top and bottom line. Continued margin expansion and positive free cash flow will be keys to DBD maintaining some of the momentum that it picked up at the end of last year. It is important to note that last year was an incredibly strong one for the company even with a weaker than expected Q1.

As part of its Q4 2025 earnings presentation, Diebold Nixdorf released the following expectations for FY 2026, and all were improvements from the company’s last forward update from an investor presentation made last year. Revenue is expected to be roughly $3.9 billion, an increase of about 2.6% from FY 2025. Free cash flow is expected to be in a range of $255 million to $270 million as DBD targets a 50% conversion rate or better from its EBITDA. Its range of adjusted earnings is projected to average $5.50 per share, a 22% improvement from FY 2025 once its tax benefits are removed.

Conclusion

Diebold Nixdorf has been on an incredibly strong run over the last year with its stock up almost 130% in the last 12 months. However, at its current price, DBD is a tech stock with a forward PE below 15 and a PEG ratio well below 1. This still sounds like a good deal to me and a reason to maintain my Buy rating.

data.title: ASML Q1 Earnings Preview: Strong Growth Tailwinds, But Valuation Stretched
data.description:
Article Thesis

ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) will report its next earnings results on April 15. In this article, I will take a look at what investors can expect from the upcoming report. ASML remains a company with nice growth tailwinds, but following huge gains over the last year, shares are rather pricey now, which is why I don't think ASML is a great investment at north of $1,400 per share.

Past Coverage

I have written about ASML Holding here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in January, when I published this article. I gave the company a neutral rating then, which has worked out well so far -- at the time of writing, the total return since that article was published around two and a half months ago is exactly zero. Earlier last year, I had given ASML a Buy rating, with strong gains since then, but I eventually switched to a Neutral rating as ASML got more expensive, which weakened its total return outlook to some degree. With ASML's next earnings coming up, I want to update my views on the company and take a look at what we can expect from the next earnings report.

ASML Q1 Earnings Report: What Can We Expect?

The chip industry continues to boom on the back of strong AI investments that cause NVIDIA (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Micron (MU), and so on to experience huge revenue growth. Higher sales by these companies and their peers mean that more chips need to be produced, with Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) being a key manufacturer, which in turn means that production capacity has to grow. That means more of ASML's lithography machines are needed -- so ASML is a clear beneficiary of the current AI investment wave as well. This is reflected in the current estimates for ASML's upcoming earnings report, as we can see here: Analysts believe that ASML will report a revenue increase of 14% to 15% next week, which is a pretty good growth rate, although less than the average over the last couple of quarters. It is, I believe, important to note that ASML tends to outperform expectations, though -- the company has beaten the revenue consensus estimate in 13 out of the last 16 quarters. History thus suggests that ASML will likely report a growth rate that is a little better than currently expected, although this isn't guaranteed, of course. Looking at estimates for the following quarters, we see that analysts believe that growth will accelerate, with Q2 revenue growth being forecasted at 18% and with Q3 revenue growth being forecasted at a very nice 29%, although this is partially due to a rather easy comparison due to Q3 2025 being a relatively weak quarter. Still, Q1 could be the very solid beginning of a year during which ASML will see some growth acceleration, which would be nice.

When it comes to profits, analysts are forecasting nice growth as well, although the expected earnings per share increase isn't outstanding, at 13% more compared to last year's first quarter. This is a little less than the expected revenue growth rate, which naturally isn't great -- ideally, we would want to see net profits grow a little faster than revenues due to margin increases thanks to operating leverage, with earnings per share growing a little faster than net income on top of that due to buybacks. But Wall Street analysts believe that some margin compression will cause ASML to report a slightly weaker earnings per share growth rate compared to its revenue growth rate. ASML has guided for a gross margin of 51% to 53% for the first quarter during the Q4 conference call -- at the midpoint of the guidance range, that's down 200 basis points from the 54% level seen in Q1 2025. It thus makes sense that analysts believe that margins could be lower in the upcoming report. ASML's management explained that their not very strong gross margin guidance was due to factors such as product mix and weaker expected servicing results. The good news is that management also explained that these factors wouldn't persist forever -- they see better margins in 2027, and their goal for 2030 is a gross margin of 57% at the midpoint, so meaningful improvements could be coming over the next couple of years. If that happens, ASML could see its earnings per share growth easily outpace its revenue growth beyond 2026. In the past, analysts oftentimes were too conservative with their earnings per share estimates, with 15 earnings per share consensus beats over the last 16 quarters. Like with ASML's revenues, we could thus see another beat when ASML reports next week, I think.

Investors also will be keen to hear what management has to say about Q2: The revenue guide will be important, and so will be ASML's gross margin guidance. Management may hint at gross margin improvements starting during 2026 already, which would be a nice development, although that is far from guaranteed -- it may take a couple more quarters until this metric improves meaningfully. Cash generation and usage will also be an important item to look for in the company's earnings report. ASML generated around $13 billion in free cash flow over the last year, so a little more than $3 billion per quarter. I expect that the Q1 report will show a nice cash flow number as well -- and maybe ASML has put that money to work via its $12 billion share repurchase program. While shares aren't cheap right now, which limits the effectiveness of share repurchases, the company has likely still bought back some shares on top of paying its recently increased dividend.

Is ASML Holding A Good Investment?

ASML is the clear leader in its industry, and global chip demand has been growing nicely for years. Unless the AI boom ends suddenly, chip demand will very likely grow in the coming years as well. This makes for a nice growth backdrop for ASML, I believe, but investors should, of course, not only consider a company's growth outlook.

Based on current estimates, ASML Holding trades for around 41x net earnings right now, resulting in an earnings yield of 2.4% or so. This makes for a rather pricey valuation, I believe, both in absolute terms and also compared to the valuations of many other growth companies with similar AI exposure: Broadcom, NVIDIA, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company all trade at considerably lower valuations compared to ASML. One can argue that ASML benefits from broad industry growth, no matter what company ultimately has the best chips, but that holds true for TSM as well -- and yet, TSM is way cheaper, trading at roughly 25x forward earnings.

Factoring in all of that, I'm neutral on ASML Holding for now. The company definitely has advantages such as its strong market position and positive growth outlook, but the stock looks fully priced -- I think upside potential is rather limited following the huge gains of almost 140% over the last year. It's better to buy into such stocks when they are comparatively inexpensive, such as last fall below $1,000, but I'm not interested in buying this close to the all-time high at a 40+ times earnings multiple.

data.title: Netflix Q1 Earnings Preview: With The WBD Saga Over, Focus Shifts To Advertising & Live Sports
data.description:
Introduction

Netflix Inc. (NASDAQ: (NFLX)) is scheduled to report its Q1 earnings on Thursday, 16 April. In this article, I explore the key factors that investors need to focus on in the company’s Q1 report.

A Recap of Q4 Results

NFLX had a strong finish to the year, comfortably beating on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. More specifically, Q4 revenues came in at $12.05 billion, up 17.6% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $83.91 million. Diluted EPS came in at $0.56, up 27.3% and beating analyst estimates by $0.01.

For the first quarter, management projected revenues to come in at $12.16 billion, which would translate to a growth of 15.3% y/y. Diluted EPS is expected to come in at $0.76, which would translate to a y/y growth of 15.1%. Finally, the company’s operating margins are projected to come in at 32.1%, which would represent an expansion of 40 bps y/y [or 0.4%].

Q1 Expectations

According to LSEG Data, analysts expect Q1 revenues to come in at $12.18 billion, which would represent a y/y growth of 16%. Non-GAAP EPS is expected to come in at $0.76, which would imply a y/y growth of 15%.

The company, according to LSEG data, has had a strong track record when it comes to beating revenue estimates, as evidenced by the fact that the stock has beaten revenue estimates in nine of the last ten quarters, with the magnitude of positive surprise coming in at a decent 0.63%, on average. Even in the quarter that it missed estimates, revenues were largely in line with analyst estimates. I expect NFLX to continue its track record of beating analyst estimates in Q1 given the strong content slate, driven by the likes of Season 7 of Black Mirror.

Average Positive Surprise

0.63%

Average Negative Surprise

-0.03%

Quarter

The streaming giant’s track record of beating analyst estimates also remains strong, with the company beating in eight of the last ten quarters. On average, the magnitude of positive surprise beat remains at a healthy 6.58% on average. While the magnitude of negative surprise is higher on average, this is heavily skewed by the one quarter where the company missed estimates by 15.74%. Even this quarter was an outlier given that the earnings miss was primarily due to a one-off Brazilian tax expense, which makes it an outlier. The bar, in my opinion, has been set at a reasonable level but demanding level. As such, any miss, even if it’s due to one-off items, is unlikely to be forgiven by the street, especially since the stock is entering the earnings print with a strong momentum.

Finally, as far as the market reaction goes, despite having a positive reaction post earnings, on average, in the last ten quarters, the last three quarters has seen the stock tumble post earnings. However, heading into the earnings print, the stock has taken a significant decline as a result of the turbulence surrounding the (WBD) acquisition. Now that there is clarity on that front, investor sentiment on the stock has largely turned positive. Having said that, with the stock still down 17% in the last six months, there is still a long way to go before the stock reaches its prior highs. As such, a strong print and any positive commentary during the call should provide the necessary impetus for a positive post-earnings reaction. This is further evidenced by the improving momentum in the stock in the last three months.

Investor Focus Must Be on Ad Revenues & Live Sports

In NFLX’s Q4 letter to shareholders, management had talked in detail about the WBD acquisition and how it would help the company to “accelerate its business strategy.” Investors were less than pleased about the acquisition, as evidenced by the stock tumbling nearly 18% from the day the acquisition announcement was made until the day that the company announced that they were walking away.

Since the WBD saga came to an end, NFLX shares have surged, with a 10% surge immediately post the termination, once again highlighting just how relieved investors were that the company did not enter a bidding war with (PSKY).

Now that there is no acquisition-related overhang, the focus now shifts to the company’s core growth catalysts, which in my opinion, are advertising and its content & engagement.

With respect to advertising, investors, in my opinion, need to focus on whether revenues will maintain their trajectory towards achieving management’s target of approximately $3 billion for FY26, roughly double the figure achieved in 2025. Furthermore, investors should also focus on whether management offers revised targets for ad revenues in 2026, which would signal whether the company has corrected its course trajectory after a minor dip in Q4, as evidenced by the ad revenue growth of 79.1% y/y missing analyst expectations of 81.5% y/y. Any colour on the ad tier’s fill rates should also be watched by investors given that management is targeting a fill rate of approximately 70% in 2026, a significant jump from the 45% figure it achieved in 2025.

The growth in advertising revenues would also be reliant on how much progress NFLX makes on bringing more live sports to the platform. The company has already gained exclusive rights to the MLB’s Opening day, Home Run derby and the Field of Dreams games for the 2026 season and is looking to expand its relationship with NFL by adding more games ahead of the new season. The expansion of NFL partnership is key, in my opinion, to maintaining the growth trajectory of its advertising business. As such, management commentary on this aspect is yet another area that investors should focus on to gain more perspective about the long term growth prospects of the ads business.

Finally, while the company has once again raised prices across all its subscription plans last month for the first time since January 2025, the impact would not be felt at least until the second quarter. However, it would also be vital for investors to see just how the company is planning to invest the additional revenues into its content, since this would also give them an idea about the trajectory of the ads business.

Valuation

I used a forward P/E multiple approach together with a bottom-up FY26 Profit & Loss build to compute my price target for NFLX. The valuation is directly tied to the thesis surrounding NFLX’s advertising business since the ads business is the primary growth catalyst for NFLX, in my opinion, going forward. I utilized NFLX management’s guidance and used the ad revenue growth assumption as the deciding variable across all three scenarios [bear, base, bull], in order to isolate the variable driving my investment thesis [ad revenue growth trajectory], while holding constant the company’s subscription business assumption.

NFLX management forecast FY26 revenues to come in between $50.70 and $51.70 billion [midpoint is $51.20 billion) and FY26 operating margins to come in at 31.5%. Critically, the operating margin guidance was made when NFLX was still in the running for WBD acquisition and it explicitly included about $275 million in acquisition-related expenses tied to WBD. Given that NFLX has walked away from the deal, the expenses no longer matter. Subsequently, adjusted FY26 operating margins would then be forecast to come in at approximately 32%.

NFLX management expect the company’s ad revenues to hit approximately $3 billion in FY26. I have assumed a 70% incremental operating margin on ad revenue after taking into account the fact the NFLX has already built its in-house Ads Suite infrastructure. This margin is held constant across all three scenarios.

I then constructed a 3x3 valuation matrix that combines the three FY26 EPS scenarios with the three forward P/E scenarios. The forward P/E multiples under each scenario are as follows:

Bear Case 25x: The multiple corresponding to NFLX’s 52-week low, reached during the WBD deal saga

Base Case 31x: The current median forward P/E, post the WBD deal saga, which reflects the stable trading band since NFLX walked away from the deal

Bull Case 37x: A re-rating scenario under which the market prices NFLX once again at its pre-deal premium

The base case target of $99.20 are the levels around which NFLX is currently trading at. Moreover, the risk/reward ratio [base case upside/bear case downside] is nearly 0, far below the 1:1 threshold that signals accumulation.

Furthermore, when one looks at the tail asymmetry, it is interesting to note here that the bull case upside of about 23.9% is roughly symmetric with the bear case downside of 22.6%, which results in a tail R/R of approximately 1.06. This implies that NFLX stock heads into the earnings with a balanced risk profile with no directional skew to lean on pre-earnings.

Taking all of this together, the quant framework implies that NFLX is fairly valued at current levels. As such, I am downgrading my rating on the stock from a BUY to a HOLD.

I do want to add a caveat to my rating downgrade: it is not a reflection of my view on the long-term growth prospects of NFLX. On the contrary, as a NFLX shareholder, I continue to hold my shares and do believe that the fundamentals of the company look strong. The downgrade merely reflects the fact that at current levels, the stock is already pricing in the Q1 ad-revenue growth scenario, leaving no cushion for any disappointment. The risk/reward becomes more favourable at the mid-$80s. Alternatively, should the management revise its ad-revenue target to the upside, then the valuation framework needs to be visited as the R/R would become more favourable. For now, heading into the Q1 print, a HOLD rating is warranted.

Concluding Thoughts

It is set to be an interesting Q1 print from NFLX, the first since the post-WBD saga. The stock is entering the print with a strong momentum and as such, it is imperative that the company delivers a strong report, one that comfortably beats both the top- and bottom-line estimates.

The key focus area for investors, in my opinion, would be the progress made by the advertising segment, and whether the likes of MLB deals provide the management with a sufficient reason to revise their advertising revenue guidance to the upside. Any additional commentary on the NFL package would also help investors to have a sense of how the advertising revenue trajectory would look like in the long run.

From a valuation perspective, I am downgrading my rating from a BUY to a HOLD purely because my quant framework offers no meaningful cushion for disappointment. At current levels, the stock is fairly valued and given the positive momentum it has, any miss or downbeat guidance would be a headwind. For investors looking to enter the stock, I would either wait for a pullback to the mid-$80s or if the management revises their ad revenue targets upward in the Q1 print.

The WBD saga is over and NFLX investors are grateful for it. The focus should now be on whether the streaming giant can maintain its throne on its own, via advertising and live sports.
